title: Graph Representation
description: The overview before Adjacency Matrix and Adjacency List: two fundamentally different ways to actually store a graph in code.
objectives: Understand why a graph needs an explicit representation, unlike a tree's simple Node struct
Preview the two main representations: adjacency matrix and adjacency list
Know the basic space trade-off between them
Get oriented before the two dedicated deep-dive topics

sections.text: 1. Recap: Trees Had an Obvious Structure

A TreeNode was simple: exactly a left pointer and a right pointer, built directly into the struct. A graph vertex can connect to any number of other vertices — there's no fixed number of pointer fields that would work for every graph. We need a different, explicit way to store "who connects to whom."

A graph representation is the data structure used to store which vertices are connected to which, since a graph's connections can't be hard-coded into a fixed struct the way a binary tree's left/right can.

rows: A 2D grid — matrix[i][j] tells you whether an edge exists between vertex i and vertex j
For each vertex, a list of exactly the vertices it directly connects to

sections.text: This is only a preview — each gets its own dedicated topic right after this one, with full implementation and a proper look at the trade-offs.
4. A Quick Taste of the Trade-off

Recall 2D Arrays for the matrix idea, and vector<vector<int>> for the list idea — both use structures we've already built. The matrix always uses space proportional to (number of vertices)², regardless of how many edges actually exist; the list only uses space proportional to the actual number of edges. Which one is better depends entirely on how densely connected the graph is — exactly what the next two topics dig into.

sections.title: A seating chart's cross-reference table vs each guest's personal invite list
sections.text: One way to record who's friends with whom at a big event is a giant grid — every guest against every other guest, marking yes/no for each pair (a matrix). Another way is giving each guest a personal list of just their actual friends (a list). If most people only know a handful of others, the personal list wastes far less paper than filling out an enormous mostly-empty grid.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming a graph needs one 'correct' representation — the right choice depends on the graph's density and what operations are needed most.
Forgetting a graph, unlike a tree, has no built-in fixed-size node structure — the representation itself IS the graph's storage.
Assuming representation choice doesn't affect algorithm performance — it does, significantly, as the next two topics show.
Conflating the graph's vertices/edges (the abstract concept) with the specific data structure used to represent them (a separate, practical choice).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why does a graph need an explicit representation, while a binary tree doesn't need anything beyond a simple Node struct?
questions.answer: Because a graph vertex can connect to any number of other vertices, with no fixed limit — unlike a binary tree's fixed left/right pointers, there's no single struct shape that would work for every graph.

questions.question: What are the two main ways to represent a graph?
questions.answer: An adjacency matrix, a 2D grid marking whether an edge exists between each pair of vertices, and an adjacency list, where each vertex stores a list of just the vertices it directly connects to.

questions.question: What is the basic space trade-off between the two representations?
questions.answer: An adjacency matrix always uses space proportional to the number of vertices squared, regardless of edge count; an adjacency list uses space proportional to the actual number of edges.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Unlike a tree's fixed left/right Node struct, a graph vertex can connect to any number of others, so it needs an explicit representation to store its connections. The two main approaches are an adjacency matrix — a 2D grid where matrix[i][j] marks whether an edge exists between vertices i and j — and an adjacency list, where each vertex stores a list of just its actual neighbors. The basic trade-off is space: a matrix always costs (vertices)² regardless of how many edges actually exist, while a list costs space proportional only to the real edge count, making it far more efficient for sparse graphs — the exact detail explored in the next two topics.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Graph Representation
sections.text: Adjacency matrix = a giant grid, everyone against everyone. Adjacency list = each vertex's own short list of actual friends. Sparse graph → list wins on space.
